reasoning: Han Duck-soo is currently the Acting President of South Korea and would be required to resign 30 days prior to the June 3 election (i.e., by May 4) in order to run. Doing so would be highly controversial, as he would be abandoning his caretaker role during a national crisis, a move without precedent in modern South Korean history (e.g., Hwang Kyo-ahn declined to run under similar circumstances in 2017). Furthermore, Han missed the April 15 deadline to participate in the People Power Party (PPP) primary, and PPP leadership has indicated that speculation about his candidacy is unhelpful. While he recently stated on April 22 that he has made "no decision yet" amidst mounting calls from desperate conservatives, his advanced age (75) and technocratic background make a sudden, independent populist run unlikely. The "no decision" likely reflects a desire to maintain political leverage or polite deflection rather than a firm intent to run. Thus, the probability remains low, heavily anchored by the strong base rate against acting presidents running for the office they are temporarily holding.
